Hi, Andi.
How are you?
Good.
How are you?
Sorry, my camera.

One second.

Yes.

I'm great.

Pretty good.
I am so excited to be talking to you.
Thank you for letting Marilia push you into Thursday meeting in the middle of the day.
I really, really appreciate it.
Of course.
No, I'm happy to get the conversation rolling.
I saw that it was on Tuesdays and Thursdays.
I'm like, oh, maybe I should expand my availability a little bit more so I could talk to Andy sooner.
Oh, yeah.
timing.
I'm like so excited.
You're the first person I've talked to about this role, but like we're in desperate need.
So really excited to get this going and to chat with you.
Where are you based?
Yes.
I live in Beverly, which is just north of Boston.
role, we're inEurope.
Um, if, if, if you've been to like the North shore area.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Awesome.

Yeah.

Yes.
I'm in Newport.
So the other sailing town on the other side of Boston.
Yeah.
I noticed actually that you're there.
My parents live in Portsmouth.
Um, and my dad does a lot of sailing.

Yeah.
So we're there in the same area.
Oh yeah.
Amazing.
Um, yeah.
I love Bristol.
used to keep our boat up there up like that river, but it's kind of like a journey if you're coming down towards Newport.
So.
Uh, yeah.

And I always forget how long.
I'm like, this is taking, like, far too long right now.
Yeah, yeah.
Amazing.
Well, lots of things in common.
Very fun.

So I can give you a little bit of a sense, like I said, you're the first person I'm chatting with, but I can give you a sense of, like, how I'm thinking about the role and kind of where the need came from.
So I've been at GrowthX for about six months.
GrowthX has been a company for a little over a year.
So still very new.
We're doing our first round of renewals right now.

So thinking a lot, like, really probably should have done this sooner, but startup life, like, starting to measure client health, look at, like, indicators that we can have that are ongoing to track those sorts of things.
While my title is head of customer ops, like, my background is in, like, CS.
And so specifically, like, I'm managing.
The directors and owning kind of the outcomes for the delivery team, which is like, like account directors where we develop AI, AI driven content, or like SEO and like, LLM discovery for companies.
So I kind of like own outcomes, CSAT, things like that for clients.
And well, just like, own those metrics across the team.

And I found myself very much in the weeds kind of trying to help directors, we're going, we're like thinking about reorganizing the way that, you know, we set up the pods and kind of the alignment of and definition of some of our roles.
And so we really need somebody that can step in and help us think a little bit more about defining the metrics.
Tricks, just, I need a right-hand person, essentially.
It's hard to be kind of, like, in the weeds, and then kind of take a step back and think strategically.
So we're doing, like, multiple things at once here.
So that's, like, where this need came from.
Yeah, that totally makes sense.
No, it's hard to be strategic and, like, rolling around in the data and the details.
It's a lot of, like, context switching to get that.
Exactly, and just, like, finding time to do it.
So looking for help in somebody that can kind of help set up the systems and, like, as we lay the roadmap, help me execute against kind of what's coming next.
Yeah.
Perfect.
So would love to get your, like, elevator pitch, and then I have a little list of questions, and we can talk about that, and then you can ask me any other questions at the end.
Yeah, of course.
You want me to jump right in?
Yeah, let's go.
Okay, so just a little bit about me.
Okay.
Okay.
I've spent the last seven years or so in a more strategic program manager role.
Previously, before joining Rapid7, I was at Accenture Federal supporting our cyber strategy practice.
In those roles, I was both a change enablement, change management leader on a contract, as well as a deputy program manager.
So directly supporting those director level positions at Accenture to basically execute against our roadmap and deliverables that we have agreed upon with our federal customers.
Fast forwarding, I knew I wanted to gain exposure to more industry leading best practices and ways to deliver since I've primarily been in the federal space my career.
And so that's what attracted me to Rapid7.
I have really enjoyed...
That intersection of strategic design thinking as well as execution.
And that's where I kind of found the sweet spot of customer success operations there in a strategy, a cyber strategy organization as well.
So I've come to really enjoy that role function and where it sits within the broader organization.
But what's leading me to kind of explore other opportunities is that the pace of Rapid7 and the culture fit, to be honest, is not best long term for me.
And I'm looking for something very much being able to operate in the gray, very hyper growth, strong collaboration environment.
So those are like a couple of things that I really enjoy in a role and kind of like what I'm looking for at my next opportunity.
Amazing.
That was going to be one of my questions, because I know Accenture, Rapid7 are kind of bigger names, but we are very much in the build phase, so that's great to hear.

At either of those companies, have you had the opportunity to build a system in customer success, or any part of the operations that you've kind of been able to own end-to-end, and can you walk me through what you did to build it out?
Yeah, of course.
In general, I have experience with both being at AFS and Rapid7.
I was the fifth person to join our contract when we won it at AFS, the first non-developer tech person.
So a lot of the more program management, operational backbone components of standing up a contract did fall onto me, and those were growth opportunities for me, honestly, being a consumer.
Sultan at that time.

So I helped play the foundation of how we delivered for that program, implementing JIRA and PI planning for the more development front, and then like Salesforce and ServiceNow for that more operational and forecasting arm of delivery also.

So where I think we would be a little bit more applicable here was that it's funny that you said one of the main like whys behind this role is you entering your new cycle of renewals and trying to drive customer health.
That is something I've been really hyper focused on most recently at Rapid7.
would think that they would be a little bit more mature with where they are in that delivery.
But I have been working with our rev ops, our data analytics and our product teams since day dot of me joining to kind of build a unified view of what.

Strong customer health indicators would look like so that we could make prioritized decisions of when customers are coming up for renewal and accurately identifying and projecting risk and how that might impact the forecast.
What have you guys settled on for those?
Some of the metrics that have informed customer health.
Yeah, I can kind of walk through.
There's a couple of buckets.
The first one being product health and adoption, like baseline, is the product set up properly?
Then where are they along that maturity curve?
Have they done those kind of nice-to-have parts of the tools that really the super users are doing?
Beyond that, looking at operationally, how have we been able to engage with them?
What do our open incidents look like?
Have we had any mis-soc detections?
And that one is like hyper-specific for cyber tools.
And then the more so are they satisfied with...
The product itself.
We do biannual NPS scores and CSAT surveys with our customer base.
So feeding in not only the most recent scoring, but also like a historical aggregation of those to see how it's trending over time.
And then not so much data driven, but we have a very much a human component titled the advisor assessment.
Our advisors are the same things as CSMs.
And it is a gut reaction, red, green, yes, no of their pulse on the account.
So we kind of blend a more human approach along with a lot of like integral data points within the product and like support aspects as well.
Yeah, awesome.
Cool.
And then, you know, you came in and since day zero at Rapid7, you said you've been working on...
And then...
Solving like client health and figuring out how to how to measure that.
How do you just thought how did you decide that that was the number one operational problem?
Are there other things that you've had to evaluate or balance as you've been there?
Yeah, that's a really good question.
Kind of two parts.
One, as part of the interview process, I heavily discussed renewals and their renewal rate, which has been declining over the past several years.
And so the CS leaders and the ops leaders had said, like, towards the end of this fiscal year, they had a goal in mind.
They had no process of how to get there.
That was really myself and my team's responsibility.
So that was definitely a stated objective come onboarding.
But also to help balance some of my other priorities.
Some of the tools like in my project management.
Toolbox I use a lot is being able to prioritize my work in a consistent way.
That wasn't something that's really been socialized broadly at Rapid7.
So within my first 60 days, I stood up an initiatives like intake, execution, closure process to help prioritize the work that was being given to me by a number of stakeholders.

I directly report to the senior vice president of customer success operations, but my customers, the other ops teams, you know, the other customer success leaders always fed me additional like work items.
So that helped me prioritize my work.
It helped increase visibility of more siloed projects across Rapid7.
And then it also helped with like cross-functional collaboration, because I knew sooner of like what projects, like with what teams.
that I'd be working on.
So that's really helped me kind of understand and sort through my priority list and my initiative backlog.
Yeah.
What tool are you using?
A couple of things.
We use Slack a lot.
So I have built a, with the IT team, we've been building a Slack workflow that connects to creating like tickets within Jira.
And I've created a Jira board because it's something that anybody within the Rapid7 environment can access.
So if they have questions about where I am in a project, they can always access that Jira board.
I'm able to report consistently and like on a regular like reporting basis out to some of the leaders.
And then I also use the Google suite for like my more like project management and like that level.
Of tooling.
I've had previous experience with Microsoft Projects and some of those other tools, but Rapid7 really uses Google as their, like, home base for everything.
Yeah, yeah, awesome.
One of the things that I think this person will do, like, in the first month or two is we've done some evaluation.
We use linear right now, and we have, like, a pretty solid linear ticket system.
Like, we're actually implementing it for the account managers, too, because right now, like, our design team, like, all our other teams are using it.
So trying to get everybody there.
Consistency.
But trying to, like, we're looking at a couple of other tools, so vitally, and, like, I just did a demo with Monday.
Yeah.

To try and figure out, like, how we think more holistically about, especially, like, capacity planning across teams, because we're, like, constantly onboarding clients with a really high touch, and so getting...
Getting a sense of where people are in projects is really important, but that would be something that this person would be responsible for.
So it's good to know that you've kind of thought about that and done some of that before.

Yeah, I also have a couple, I have experience with like vendor analysis at Accenture with when we were up for our own recompete with cyber vendors that we worked with to be delivering to the government.
So I do have experience with that.
And we've also been doing that for some of our internal tooling at Rapid7 kind of recently as well.
Yeah, awesome.
Cool.
Okay, and then I think one of the most unique parts of especially like customer ops work is that you really do sit between teams.
Like I find like the skill set here is like being a bridge or connective tissue across teams, especially like translating things to engineering teams, getting the attention.
And priority on, like, pain points from the customer team that they just, like, can't express in a different way.
Yeah, I get that.
And then getting, like, that work prioritized against, like, the  product work that they might be doing or something like that, you know?
Yeah, the cool features.
Exactly.
Everybody loves those.
But it's like, fix what you already built.
Oh, yeah.
No matter the company.
That is not a unique problem.
Yeah.
I almost feel like the bigger the company, the harder it is.
Like, at Uber, it was impossible.
But can you talk me through a time when you had to work through conflicting priorities across teams to, like, get something across the finish line?
Yeah, of course.
While I was at Accenture in the cycle of where I was deputy program manager, our product owner at the time went on to extended leave.
So I filled in as product owner.
gonna
For about six months or so, and I really ramped up of what that meant to be a product owner, and kind of what you said, I felt the core function of that role was being a bridge and a connector.
So part of my requirements gathering and prioritization was really understanding customer sentiment and being and advocating the voice of the customer, such as those unsexy things.

Like, for example, one of our customers had a real issue with our file size capacity, and that was articulated across a number of the customer base, but that was never directly, like, raised from our clients as a prioritized requirement to our development team.
So I have been in multiple situations where I've had to advocate for customers by listening to them through these.
Sessions, capturing what they're saying, and then kind of translating that back to something to the developers.
When it's conflicting, I always try to lead with the why behind it.
And I think that that is one of the best ways to help communicate across teams that have different priorities and focus areas.
Because something as simple as file size capacity isn't a real empowering thing for a developer to work on.
But when I was able to articulate the why, and I was able to say, like, X amount of customers have run into these issues.
And because of this, then they can't do that.
And kind of leading and providing a little background and context and value setting is where I found the most success with cross-functional collaboration.
Totally.
Yeah.
Okay.
Well, those are all of the questions I've had.
We got through that really quickly, but those were great examples.
Sorry if I was talking like a million miles a minute.
No, it's great.
That's perfect.
I mean, we book 45, but usually like, I mean, I've been doing a lot of interviews for a couple of different roles.
So usually they take around 30.
So this is not out of the normal.
Yeah.
But we have plenty of time for you to ask me questions.
So what can I answer for you?
Yes.
So at the top of the meeting, you said that for a lot of the customers, you're entering that renewal phase.
How could you tell me a little bit about how your contracts are currently structured with your with your customers and kind of like that type of deal size, just so I can get a better understanding?
Yeah.
So we have about, we've just over 50 customers today, I believe.
And the interesting thing about growthx is we are like a services agency model.
We're built on an internal platform that we eventually will, will.

will become client-facing and we will be more product-led, but today with services, it's, and so there's like a whole nother, that's a whole nother project that will come in 2026, but with the way that the services, the contracts are built and because like, you know, this was started with our CEO founder is like very much the persona, he is the brand in a lot of ways, so a lot of these deals were like, he was a CMO at a bunch of big companies, he brought in like other CMO friends to pay him kind of like consulting work, so a lot of our original contracts are on three-month rolling basis, so our goal right now is to turn those into annual contracts, which we've had pretty good success with so far, and the contract size to start was around averaging around $8,000 a month, we're up to 15 now, 15 a month now, so has increased pretty significantly,
Over the year.
And I think, like, we're adding additional work streams.
Like, we're just defining new lanes of what we can offer.
So I think flat renewals getting to annual is, like, our baseline goal right now.
And we're seeing fair success with that.
Like, a couple of his personal friends are like, no, no, we'll keep it, like, quarterly rolling.
And they're being cute.
But I think the sentiment there is pretty positive.
And then, you know, we'll think about expansion once we have, like, a solid baseline.
Okay, that makes sense.
And how I know a big part of this role would be defining customer health metrics and assessing risk.
At what, like, level are you all doing that currently today?
Yeah, so we're looking at two things right now.
We've set up some pretty clear ICP metrics first to, like, figure out...
Okay, as we're measuring health, are these customers that we want or not?
Because we said yes to everyone, obviously, in the first six months, common problem.
Now you can take here.

Yeah, so we have, like, we now have an ICP score for every client, and we're looking at relationship adherence to, like, deliverables in the contract, because we are literally public, like, five blog posts a week on average, you know, very easy to measure.
And then, quality of the output, which we have, like, a chief content officer who's auditing content, and then performance.
So, you know, when we're publishing that content, are they getting clicks, impressions, is it leading to qualified leads, things like that.
So those are the, the score, that's the scoring we're looking at.
So, today...

So like a lot of that, it's like self-reported, like quality is like still pretty subjective based on, you know, it's random audits, you know, and the clients kind of self-reporting on what they think the quality is.
Relationship, obviously, that's always going to be difficult to like put a number to.
But the other pieces I think are relatively easy, but it's just like, are we delivering or not?
And then tracking against performance.
I think we can do a better job.
We're building out dashboards, but we could do a better job of like keeping on top of like progress against goal on an ongoing basis.
Yes, that is what my biggest like challenge and something I've been working towards is like standing up these dashboards.
It's a hard thing.
It's being able to like measure against them and then act on.
building we're building then
What is, like, what is the output of that?
And making sure that the customer success leaders are able to enable their teams.
I get that.
Yeah, like, are you having the conversation on day one?
Are you having, like, are you having the conversation continually on where are we aiming?
Should we be changing that and changing course a little bit?
That's also one of the reasons we're doing a bit of a reorg and thinking about, like, who's focused on what aspects of a partnership at any given point of time.
There's two to three people supporting any given client, and then their, like, person is supporting, like, three to five clients, but they're doing different jobs.
So, like, we have client deliverables.
So we have a team that's doing, that's, like, publishing.
And then we have, I think we're going to have a team of just, like, CS sort of job title instead of, like, editorial customer.

You know, we were trying to have, like, that mid-layer do too much, so just, like, CS project manager, and then have, like, a director who can do that more strategic work and build those relationships.
Yeah.
Okay, that makes sense.
That was going to be one of my questions, was how the customer success, like, organization itself was currently organized, and where do you see it evolving in the future?
But that makes sense to me.
Yeah.
So, I guess, a little bit more, like, culture-wise, I saw that you have also come from larger companies.
What was kind of, like, your why for joining, and, like, a couple months ago?
Yeah, it's interesting when you talk about your role at Accenture, what I was doing at Uber was very similar.
I was in an incubator within Uber.
So, essentially, we were the first team that was running government contracts for, like, Uber trips.
So like, very similar problems, I think.
Yes, I'm sure.

We had our own budget, you know, we were operating as our as like a totally independent unit within Uber, like we were leveraging the tech, but very little else we were building our own like, interface for clients, things like that.
So I got that like taste for that independence.
And then from there, I went to another small startup, Tango, where they had like a lot of Uber, an Uber alum was one of the founders.
So that's how I ended up there.
Also, Series A, I like the building.
I like the ambiguity.
I think I will say like, this is, Tango was, it was like Harvard Business School, like kids that were all like, this is what the playbook is.
There is no playbook here.
But everybody here has actually seen the play before in the startup world.
And so it's a lot more freedom to experiment with models.
They do take expertise into account, but it's not like, what does the handbook say?
So I think that's more fun.
It's also like, there's a lot of, you know, we're doing a reorg right now.
So like, there's a lot of adjusting to that piece.
But like, I just, I think there's so much like, freedom and opportunity and the excitement around the, the way that we're building this company in terms of like, we don't need a series B right now.
Like, we're gonna, we're gonna look for one because if like, who knows when they're gonna stop giving out money for AI.
But like, we are today, like, I think last month we ended it.
So it's really nice to know that we're funding our own growth on that side as well.
I was saying yesterday, I think the thing that energizes me the most is being able to operate in the gray.
And I got a taste of that, being able to set up this contract.
It's kind of like a smaller version of this, but set up that contract from the ground up.
And I appreciated it.
And I love having that, like the latitude to be able to make those decisions.
And I appreciated my time at Accenture.
think it gave me the foundation to be able to be like a program manager.
But I think moving into something a little bit more gray and fluid and no playbook is something where I would want to be taking my next step too.
Yeah.
I think one of the other exciting pieces of this role is like in ops, it's hard to know, like, you know, management and growth opportunity.
Tune in.
Because you're just like, operating so clearly, like I said, we're going to launch a product, there's like so much work to do in there.
And we're not afraid to like, buy the tools, spend, spend money on people like we're going to hire a data analyst at the same time, like that's the next role that I'm going to set up here.
So like, we want it to be successful.
we want to be able to, like, the team, we've been hiring like crazy all fall.
So I think, and still, you know, making money.
So that's, it's nice.
And there's a there's opportunity for sure.
That's, that's great to hear.
And I guess, just one final question.
When you joined, since this was a net new role, what created for you?
What were some of your like immediate priorities and initiatives that you executed?
Like this first half?
Yeah, so I did a lot of capacity planning.
Just in terms of, like, you know, we hadn't really looked at the, we were throwing people at the problem.
We hadn't looked at the cost per person in relation to, like, what the contract value was.
And Bridget has been holding me to, like, thinking about that scalability, even as, like, closer to the people.
And I'm like, but they need help.
She's like, no, we have to think about ways to, like, scale really effectively.
So that has taken up so much of my time.
Just, like, where do we move people and resources so that we can, like, still deliver the best product while making sure that we're doing it efficiently?
So, like, that's something that I think I would want this person to have a good handle on and be able to step in and support me doing, too.
Which is why, like, that project management planning tool is going to be, like, really key for that.
so, yeah.
Thank
Like, I've been, I spent this in just, like, an org chart, moving people around and figuring things out.
Cool.
That was a lot of the questions.
Thank you for that.
I appreciate, like, kind of how candid and how open you are with talking about where you want to be heading.
Yeah, of course.
Is there anything I can clarify, either, like, from my discussion yesterday or kind of what we chatted about?
No, I really enjoyed this conversation.
I'm going to message Bridget.
I think she's the other person you'll be talking to.
Okay.
Yeah, I'm excited to keep this moving.
Great.
It was great meeting you.
I hope you have a great rest of your day.
I, we have the same crummy weather, so I hope you're doing something not outside.
No sailing today.
Yeah, exactly.
All right.
Well, thank you, Emily.
was great to meet you, and I'll talk to you soon.
Thank you.
Bye, Andy.
